speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in Ghetto Jezow. It is now 1943-- probably the spring of 1943. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Again, I am sort of reunited-- not in so many words-- but I am reunited with my cousin, the youngest son of Jacob-- and my Uncle Jacob's wife. And they were also in the-- in the ghetto of Jezow. We did not live in the same place. Because they lived-- the mother and her son lived in one apartment. I lived with a group of people in another apartment. But we saw each other daily. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must say that she was very kind. Whenever she could help me in any way with food, she always was there to help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I got into-- we all were assigned work details outside the ghetto. This was rather good. Why was it good?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job, at the time, was to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be what they call a clothing and bedding selector. And now, this was clothing and bedding that was taken away from Jews that were previously sent away to-- either it was Treblinka or Majdanek, or any of the other places. And that was-- it was-- it was all packed into a warehouse, whereby it was all together now. Our job-- my job, together with some other people-- was to take select clothing with clothing, bedding with bedding, shoes with shoes, and so on and so forth. Now, one day, while selecting the bedding-- the pillows-- I felt the pillow. And I felt, in the pillow, there was some hard object. I wasn't supposed to do it, but I opened up the pillow. And in the feathers, I found a gold coin-- which was hidden by, obviously, a Jewish family, hoping that they'll be able to use it sometimes. Now, I didn't know to whom it belonged. But I knew one thing-- it did not belong to the Germans. So I have taken this coin, hid it away as well as I could. We were warned, in case we do find something of value, we were to turn it off to the German authorities immediately. If we don't, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at best, we could get 25 lashes. At worst, we could be killed. Knowing the value of a gold coin, I wasn't about to turn it over to the German authorities. It was small. It was relatively easy to hide. And I says, what the heck. Let me try it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hide it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I must have hidden somewhere in my underwear. [LAUGHS] But I have hidden it. They did not have metal detectors in those days, fortunately for me. And of course, going through when we were leaving, they were searching us. They were frisking us, and so on and so forth. But luckily for me, they did not find the gold coin. As soon as I came back to the ghetto, I was befriended the people there that I lived with. As a matter of fact, one man happened to be a father of a very close friend of mine from Gliwice. And I've told him in confidence, look, I have found this gold coin. So this is good. He says, let me-- give me that-- show me the gold coin, and I will secure some food for you. Which he did. He take-- taken that coin, and through outside contacts, he has secured some food. Of course, I have given some of it to him, because, first of all, he was a very nice person. He was a nice man. And then, of course, I believed that he is instrumental in securing food. He is entitled to get a certain part of it. And he's gotten the best deal for me, under the circumstances at that time. And I have also made sure that any time I'm needed outside, I should be the one to select bedding. Because this has now become a sort of a gold mine. After all, you select bedding, there is a very good possibility that you will find gold coins in those bedding. Also, I might add, a friend of mine found a gold-- a gold coin in some of the clothing, in sleeves, gold coins. In Poland, they used to make jackets and suits which had padded shoulders. Now, the first thing they looked for, I mean, our friends, they looked in the padding. There was a possibility that there might be a gold coin in the padding. Also, the people that were working in shoes, they were trying to see if they-- to rip off the sole from shoes, because it is a good possibility that there might be coins in-- there were double soles. And some of the coins-- some of the shoes, basically, were-- were-- were constructed so to hide coins. We had shoemakers in the ghetto. And they-- and they were able to construct shoes which had double sole with some space in between, whereby you could hide valuables. Unfortunately, as it later turned out to be, when you did get to a concentration camp, all your clothing was taken away from you. So whatever you had in your shoes or otherwise was gone, anyway. But in the ghetto, as we were selecting these things-- now subsequently, on a later date, I one more time was able to find another gold coin. And again, we went through the same thing. And we got some more food, which lasted us for-- for a while. Now, I was-- now we are-- and that was going on. I wasn't always working at the same detail. I was working in other details, as well. I was now working-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, I think that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming to the early fall of 1943-- by early fall, I mean probably around September-- they needed-- the Jewish committee decided-- they did not decide, but they got an order from the Germans that they have to send a group of people to a labor camp, which was, I would say, about an hour away from Jesow. The name of that labor camp was Pustkow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pustkow was a small concentration camp. Basically, really, up to this day, I don't know the difference between a labor camp and a concentration camp. I presume a labor camp was a camp which did not have a crematoria. Concentration camps-- the large ones-- always had a crematoria. But this one was-- and we'll call it-- I will call it a labor camp. Basically, the treatment and everything else was identical with a concentration camp. By the way, that labor camp in Pustkow was rather feared. Because it was notorious. It was very, very, a very bad camp. There were certain camps which are known, at the time, as good camps-- so-called good camps. And there were some which were called very bad camps. Now, Pustkow had the reputation of being a bad camp. Primarily, it was known as a bad camp because the Jewish administration in Pustkow was a bunch of tyrants. And they were Jews, mind you-- they were Jews. And they were tyrants. Very rude, very, very rough individuals. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At the time when we were-- when we were sent away from Jezow, when they needed us, we did not know that we were going to Pustkow. Had I known that I was going to Pustkow, I would have tried my best to somehow get away-- try to go somewhere else. But not to Pustkow. But we didn't know that we were going to Pustkow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they loaded us up one day. They loaded us up on the truck. And an hour later, we arrived in Pustkow. Well, you can well imagine my feeling when I found out that was in Pustkow. That was the feared camp of Pustkow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the administration-- that was the Jewish administration in Pustkow-- there was-- the head of the camp was a German Jew-- inside the camp was a German Jew. And I will call him-- not I will call him Poldi, but his name was Poldi. Leopold, really, but his name was Poldi. A young Jewish man. His reputation was quite bad as a tyrant. Subsequently, as I was there, he was not a tyrant at all. He was very, very fair-minded. He was a decent human being. Whenever he struck somebody, whenever he beat up another prisoner, it was done to prevent the German from coming in and killing him. Let us say, for instance-- I'll give you a typical example. A prisoner was found, an inmate. I shouldn't really say prisoner. We were inmates. A Jewish inmate was working somewhere outside in the kitchen. And he was caught stealing potato in the kitchen, stuffing his pockets, and bringing them into the camp. Now, these potatoes in no way were taken from other prisoners. These potatoes-- they were working in the German kitchen. They were really potatoes-- which there was no shortage of potatoes at the time. But when he was caught at the gate coming in, as soon as he was caught, the Germans-- oh, we caught an inmate with potatoes. Poldi came running over to the German SS. He says, Herr Sturmbannfuhrer-- or whatever his name was for-- please, do not dirty your hands on this dog. You let me handle this dog. And he took him aside. And in full sight of everybody else, started lashing him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then, he said to the German, to the SS man, Herr Sturmbannfuhrer, do you think that he has enough punishment? So the German said, yes. I think you did justice. I don't think he will ever, ever steal potatoes by himself, he says. And then, he added, I would have really-- it would have been much easier for me to take a gun and shoot him. But you did a very good job. So I don't think he will never do it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it was obvious that this Poldi saved many lives this way. However, some people didn't understand it. They thought that he was really a tyrant. Now, there was another man there, and his name-- by the name-- also a Jewish man. He was working under Poldi. And his name was Munschni. His reputation was also very bad. But again, I found him to be-- he was a little rough. But he was really also trying to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was trying for the SS not to get involved. And he was giving sort of punishment, trying to save people's lives. So it wasn't-- the reputations that were outside the camp were really not always true. In some cases, these people were rough. But they tried to save somebody's life by giving them a few-- a few whacks, but they could really survive. Now, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in that camp of Pustkow, we were doing various jobs-- shoe repairs, clothing repairs, things like this just to keep us busy. One day, while being in Pustkow-- and we are now in the winter months of 1943. It must've been December or thereabouts. A group of German SS came to the camp and made an announcement. They are looking for volunteers [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for a special work detail. They need young men who are able to work. And the job is probably no more than for a week or thereabouts. And they will be very well-rewarded with ample food, extra rations, special clothing, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they assembled us all in the assembly. in the-- there was like-- they used to call it the appel-platz, which means where you gather every morning and you have a headcount. They gathered us there. And they said, well, whoever wants to, we are looking for-- I think, it was somewhere around 30 or 35 people out of, maybe, 200 or so, 250. Whoever wants those special rations and doesn't mind working for a week, please step aside to the left or wherever it was. Now, being young-- and extra rations, extra clothing, this sounded like paradise to me. So I stepped aside and decided, why not? I mean, look, I've been working anyway. Might as well work for extra rations. They have gotten probably close to 30 people already. And people were still coming and, suddenly, something on the inner me told me, says, what are you doing here? Why are you volunteering? Get back-- don't volunteer. And I was watching the German SS. And as soon as I saw that they were looking the other way, I quickly ran back to the main group. And says, why-- what do you need it for? Why do you want to volunteer for anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of the people went over. Well, finally, they did have enough. They loaded them up on trucks. They took them away. Subsequently, we found out these people we used as target practice for the SS. We-- we never saw them again. We never heard from them. The local Polish population told us later on that they were taken to a forest, and used as target practice. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So as fate would have it, something in the inside of me said, get out of there. Get back where you belong. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Good. Now we're back. All right. What was that inner voice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that inner voice?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the inner voice? I'll never know. I'll never know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somebody up there was watching for me. [CRYING] Somebody was looking out for me. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] We are still in Pustkow. We are now in the winter of 1943. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They have decided that Pustkow has too many inmates. They don't need so many Pustkow people anymore, Jews in Pustkow. We were only Jews in that camp. In some camps, we were mixed with Gentiles. Excuse me. This one was strictly Jews. There was a camp which was a Pustkow camp for Polish Gentiles. Now, the Polish Gentiles that were in the Polish part of Pustkow camp were people who were guilty of some infractions. I don't know how serious. But there were black marketeers. There were, maybe, thieves, maybe even people that were caught in the Polish resistance. But minor people from the Polish resistance. They were put into that camp. Now, we were not, of course, permitted to receive any food packages. But the Polish Gentiles were permitted to receive [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food packages from outside. And I do remember, one Christmas, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poldi-- which was our-- the feared Poldi which was really not-- the feared Poldi, which was really a very nice man. Although his reputation was not, but he really was a nice man-- somehow got through to the Germans, to the German SS, that they should really take all these packages that came in, and give some of these packages to the Jews, being that it was Christmas-- in the spirit of Christmas. And he prevailed. I don't know. He was really a German. He didn't look like a Jew. He was Jewish. I mean, he was known as a Jew. And they knew he was Jewish. But he was really so outspoken and-- that he somehow convinced the SS to do it. So one Christmas, we got a gift. We had food , which was not ours. But we got part of that food. And boy, what a celebration that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, now-- coming back to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the package?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God-- you name it-- ham, bread, cake. Things that we didn't see for three years, or thereabouts. You know? It was really great. This was Christmas packages which people sent to their-- that mothers and sisters and brothers, whoever there was-- sent to the Poles that were imprisoned in that camp. And it really didn't make that much of an effect. Because they could possibly get it-- almost once a month, they were allowed to bring something packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they really, it didn't really matter that much. Plus the fact that their food supply was far superior to our food supply. Because we were on the bottom of the totem pole-- Jews and all. Right? [LAUGHS] Now-- How much did you get of the food package?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you get of the food package?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what it was, really, it was sort of all pulled together. And it was sliced up in a fair way. And every prisoner got something. You got a little bit of this and a little bit of that. It wasn't a hell of a lot. But it was a treat. [LAUGHS] Let's say-- let's say, it was a treat. It helped. Even though it was a one-time occurrence, but it helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other things helped to keep you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the thing was that while you were in either camp-- either in a labor camps or in a concentration camp-- you had to form a partnership with another inmate. What was the partnership? The partnership was that-- it was sort of an honor system. But it was nonetheless a very effective honor system. When he worked outside, and he could somehow get some extra food, the idea was not to eat it, to bring it in and share it with you. And you did the same when you were working outside and were able to get extra food, bring it in and share it with him. Now sometimes, there was a group of two. Sometimes, there were three or four. But you could not be by yourself. You had to have another-- so to say, a partner. And that partner had to be somebody who was really honest. And once you found out that he was holding back on you, you had to get rid of him. Get rid of him, which means find another partner. That's what I mean, get rid of him. No other way. But basically, I must say that, in most cases, these partnership worked quite well. Because it was a matter of survival. So let's say we needed each other. So whenever he had the chance to get something, he brought it. Whenever I had a chance, I would share it with them. Now of course, we did not share it with anybody else. We only shared it with your partner. And of course, if there were three of us, we broke it into three parts and so on and so forth. But whenever there was-- so we always made sure-- the first thing you had to make sure is that you have a partner or an associate who will help you. And you will help him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your partners in particular as especially kind--

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically-- not really. Because as you will find out, as we go along, you will see that I was in so many different camps. That in each camp, I had different partners. And usually, it was-- you very rarely were sent out with the same people to another camp. So as you arrived in another camp, you had to find yourself another partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody sticks out? Not even one partner over all those years sticks out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, there was one in particular, which whom-- with-- appeared in some of my pictures, in my photographs. Later, I will point him out to you, you see. By the way, I tried to get in-- he did survive the war. And I tried to find him, somehow, after the war. And I wasn't successful. Oh, yes. I did find him after the war. But then, once I immigrated to the United States-- we kept in touch. And he was still in a DP camp in Germany. And then, he immigrated to Australia. And for the first two or three years, we kept in touch. But then, somehow, we lost contact of each other. But I do know that he did survive. And he knows that I survived. So we went our-- we each went our own way. We each got married. He got married in Australia. I got married in the United States, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe he'll see this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not keep up the relationship, the friendship any longer. We acquired new friends along the way. But in any event, we are now in Pustkow in 1943. It must have been somewhere in, maybe, January, February, or thereabouts. And an order came in. There were too many Jews in Pustkow. They don't need so many. And Pustkow, I might add, was a subcamp of Plaszow. Plaszow was the camp which was near Krakow. It was one of the major concentration camps in Poland at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they loaded up a group of people-- and I was among them-- put us into trucks, took us into Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we are Plaszow. And it is the spring or late winter, somewhere around February, March of 1943. We arrive in Plaszow, a huge, huge camp. That was huge. I mean, there were barracks and barracks and barracks of people. And strangely enough, at the time, there were women. There were some young children even still there. To me, this looked more-- at the time-- like a ghetto than a concentration camp. But it was a concentration camp. It had the wires. It had the-- the-- the guard houses and so on and so forth. I was-- at the time, I was assigned to work in a detail that was called [GERMAN]. [GERMAN], meaning in English, grand tailor-shop. We were making some kind of uniforms. I believe it was uniforms for the German army. Or it was uniforms for the medical corps. But we were working on making some kind of uniforms for the Germans in that [GERMAN]. Now, not being a tailor by any means, I just sort of-- I liked the job, because it was relatively an easy job, because it was an indoor job. You didn't have to freeze outside. And it was indoor. Any-- any job that you had indoor was by far preferable than being working on the outside and digging ditches, or whatever. But it was OK. And I was working there and helping along wherever I could. And I was in that-- in Plaszow for probably somewhere around three months, give or take.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change tapes.
